class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Computer Networks and the Internet
learning_objectives: Understand the layered network architecture (OSI and TCP/IP models)
Identify the role of each layer in network communication
Use network diagnostic tools: ping, traceroute, nslookup

lecture_notes.introduction: Every application you build communicates over a network. This course teaches you how networks actually work, from electrical signals on a wire to the HTTP requests your browser sends. Understanding networking is essential for building reliable distributed systems.
key_concepts: Layered architecture: physical, data link, network, transport, application
TCP/IP model vs OSI model
Packet switching vs circuit switching
Throughput, latency, bandwidth, and jitter
The end-to-end principle: intelligence at the edges, simplicity in the network
lecture_notes.demonstration: Use Wireshark to capture and analyze packets during a web browsing session, identifying HTTP, TCP, IP, and Ethernet headers at each layer

review.value: Application-specific functionality should be implemented at the endpoints, not in the network core; this keeps the network simple and general, enabling innovation at the application layer
Data is divided into packets that share network links with other traffic; no dedicated path is reserved, enabling efficient use of bandwidth but introducing variable delay

class_schedule.topic: Application Layer: HTTP, DNS, and Socket Programming
learning_objectives: Understand HTTP request/response semantics and connection management
Explain DNS resolution process and record types
Write a simple client-server application using TCP sockets
readings: Beej's Guide to Network Programming, Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: The application layer is where developers spend most of their time. HTTP drives the web, DNS translates names to addresses, and sockets are the API through which all network communication happens.
key_concepts: HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, HTTP/3: evolution of the web protocol
DNS hierarchy: root, TLD, authoritative servers, caching
DNS record types: A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT
BSD socket API: socket, bind, listen, accept, connect, send, recv
TCP vs UDP sockets: connection-oriented vs connectionless
lecture_notes.demonstration: Build a simple TCP echo server and client in Python using the socket module, then extend it to handle multiple concurrent clients
setup_requirements: Python 3.12+ or C with socket libraries
dig or nslookup for DNS queries

review.value: DNS responses include a TTL (time to live); resolvers cache results for that duration, reducing query latency and root/TLD server load. Most DNS lookups are served from cache
Server: socket -> bind -> listen -> accept -> send/recv -> close. Client: socket -> connect -> send/recv -> close. accept() blocks until a client connects, creating a new socket for that connection

learning_objectives: Explain TCP connection establishment, flow control, and congestion control
Contrast TCP and UDP and identify appropriate use cases for each
Analyze TCP behavior using packet captures

lecture_notes.introduction: TCP and UDP are the two workhorses of the transport layer. TCP provides reliable, ordered delivery at the cost of latency; UDP provides fast, best-effort delivery. Every networked application chooses between them based on its needs.
key_concepts: TCP three-way handshake: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
TCP flow control: sliding window, receiver advertised window
TCP congestion control: slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit
UDP: no connection, no reliability, no ordering, minimal overhead
Use cases: TCP for web, email, file transfer; UDP for video streaming, DNS, gaming
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capture a TCP connection in Wireshark, identify the three-way handshake, observe the sliding window growing during slow start, and trigger a retransmission by dropping a packet

review.value: TCP starts with a small congestion window (slow start), doubles it each RTT until loss occurs, then reduces the window. This probing behavior ensures TCP does not overwhelm the network while utilizing available bandwidth

In TCP, if one packet is lost, all subsequent packets are buffered until the lost packet is retransmitted; this delays the entire stream. UDP avoids this by delivering packets independently, which is why real-time applications prefer UDP

class_schedule.topic: Network Layer: IP Addressing and Routing
learning_objectives: Perform subnetting calculations with CIDR notation
Understand how routers forward packets using routing tables
Compare distance-vector and link-state routing algorithms

lecture_notes.introduction: The network layer is responsible for getting packets from source to destination across multiple networks. IP addressing and routing are the mechanisms that make global Internet communication possible.
key_concepts: IPv4 addressing: 32-bit addresses, dotted decimal notation
CIDR notation and subnet masks
Subnetting: dividing address spaces for organizational and routing efficiency
Routing tables: longest prefix match forwarding
Distance-vector (RIP, Bellman-Ford) vs link-state (OSPF, Dijkstra) algorithms
IPv6: 128-bit addresses, simplified header, no fragmentation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Subnet a /24 network into four /26 subnets, calculate the network address, broadcast address, and usable host range for each, then trace a packet through a 3-router network using routing tables

review.value: When a router has multiple matching entries for a destination, it uses the one with the longest (most specific) prefix; this enables hierarchical aggregation of routes while still allowing specific overrides

Distance-vector: each router shares its distance table with neighbors, converges slowly (count-to-infinity problem). Link-state: each router floods its link costs to all routers, each computes shortest paths independently, converges faster

learning_objectives: Understand common network attacks and their mitigations
Explain how TLS provides confidentiality, integrity, and authentication
Configure firewalls and understand network perimeter defense
readings: Kurose & Ross, Ch. 8 - Security in Computer Networks
Cloudflare Learning Center: What is TLS?

lecture_notes.introduction: Every network is hostile. Data can be intercepted, modified, or faked. Network security provides the tools to protect communication: encryption for confidentiality, MACs for integrity, and certificates for authentication.
key_concepts: Symmetric encryption (AES) vs asymmetric encryption (RSA, ECDSA)
TLS handshake: certificate verification, key exchange, encrypted channel
Certificate authorities and the chain of trust
Common attacks: man-in-the-middle, DDoS, DNS spoofing, ARP poisoning
Firewalls: packet filtering, stateful inspection, application-layer gateways
lecture_notes.demonstration: Capture a TLS handshake in Wireshark, examine the certificate exchange, verify the certificate chain, and observe that payload data is encrypted
setup_requirements: openssl command-line tool for certificate inspection

review.value: Client sends supported cipher suites; server responds with its certificate and chosen cipher; client verifies certificate chain to a trusted CA; both derive session keys; all subsequent data is encrypted and integrity-protected
No single security measure is sufficient; layered defenses (firewalls, TLS, authentication, input validation, monitoring) ensure that bypassing one layer does not compromise the entire system

questions.question: Explain the TCP three-way handshake step by step. Why is a three-way handshake necessary instead of a two-way handshake?
questions.answer: Step 1: Client sends SYN with initial sequence number X. Step 2: Server responds with SYN-ACK, acknowledging X+1 and sending its own initial sequence number Y. Step 3: Client sends ACK acknowledging Y+1. A three-way handshake is necessary because both sides need to exchange and acknowledge initial sequence numbers. A two-way handshake would only confirm that the client can reach the server, but not that the server can reach the client, leaving the server unable to verify the client received its sequence number.

rubric.key: Correctly describes all three steps with sequence numbers
Explains why three steps are needed over two

questions.question: A DNS resolver receives a query for www.example.com. Describe the iterative resolution process, listing each server queried in order.
questions.answer: 1. Client queries its local DNS resolver. 2. Resolver queries a root DNS server, which responds with the address of the .com TLD server. 3. Resolver queries the .com TLD server, which responds with the address of the example.com authoritative server. 4. Resolver queries the example.com authoritative server, which responds with the IP address for www.example.com. 5. Resolver returns the IP address to the client and caches the result.

questions.question: Given the network 192.168.10.0/24, subnet it into 8 equal subnets. For the third subnet, provide the network address, broadcast address, first usable host, last usable host, and subnet mask.
questions.answer:
8 subnets require 3 additional bits (2^3=8), so /24 becomes /27. Each subnet has 32 addresses (2^5), 30 usable hosts.

Subnet 3 (index 2, counting from 0): 192.168.10.64/27
Network address: 192.168.10.64
Broadcast address: 192.168.10.95
First usable host: 192.168.10.65
Last usable host: 192.168.10.94
Subnet mask: 255.255.255.224

rubric.key: Correct first and last usable host addresses

questions.question: Explain the count-to-infinity problem in distance-vector routing and describe one technique to mitigate it.
questions.answer: The count-to-infinity problem occurs when a link failure causes two routers to keep incrementing their distance estimates by routing through each other. For example, if the link from A to C fails, A updates its distance to C via B, but B's route to C goes through A. They keep incrementing each other's estimates until reaching infinity. Split horizon mitigates this by preventing a router from advertising a route back to the neighbor it learned it from, breaking the routing loop.

rubric.key: Correctly explains the loop causing distance increment

major_assignments.title: Reliable File Transfer Protocol
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a reliable file transfer protocol on top of UDP. Your protocol must handle packet loss, reordering, and corruption by implementing acknowledgments, retransmission, and checksums. Test it over a simulated lossy network.
objectives: Implement reliable delivery mechanisms on top of an unreliable transport
Design a protocol with sequence numbers, acknowledgments, and retransmission timers
Handle packet loss, corruption, and reordering gracefully
Analyze protocol performance under varying loss rates
deliverables: Source code for sender and receiver implementing the reliable protocol
Protocol specification document describing message formats and state machines
Performance analysis: throughput vs loss rate graph for 0%, 5%, 10%, 20% loss
Test results showing successful file transfer under all loss conditions

criteria: Protocol specification clearly defines message formats with headers
State machines for sender and receiver are well-defined
Sequence numbers and acknowledgments handle all edge cases
Checksum detects corruption reliably
Files transfer correctly with no byte errors under all loss rates
Retransmission timer handles packet loss efficiently
Protocol handles duplicate packets and out-of-order delivery
Clean shutdown with no hanging connections or lost data
Throughput analysis under varying loss rates is provided
Protocol uses go-back-N or selective repeat for efficiency
Timer values are tuned based on measured RTT
Comparison with stop-and-wait shows improvement
Protocol specification is clear and complete
Performance analysis is presented with graphs
Code is well-documented
Test methodology is described
Correct reliable file transfer over lossy UDP
Well-designed protocol with clear specification
Performance analysis demonstrating protocol efficiency
Thorough testing under multiple loss conditions

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of networking concepts and calculations

lab_assignments.description: Socket programming and network analysis labs

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all networking topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures and mandatory for lab sessions. More than 3 unexcused absences may result in a reduced participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of networking concepts is encouraged. All code and protocol designs must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying protocol implementations from the internet or other students constitutes plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a Linux environment, Wireshark, Python 3.12+ or C with socket libraries, and network access for packet capture experiments.
